prerequisites.title: Pre-Upgrade Checklist
prerequisites.summary: Telco Cloud Platform follows strict upgrade guidelines. Complete these checks before starting any component upgrade.
checklist: The existing Telco Cloud solution in production is fully functional.
The Telco Cloud Platform {target} bundle is downloaded and accessible from the Advanced downloads page.
The environment has enough storage capacity for backups and snapshots.
Management components are snapshotted and backed up (see the Snapshot & Backup phase).
All components in production are patched to the latest level.
An upgrade plan has been developed from the Upgrade Sequence — this runbook is that plan.

snapshot-backup.title: Snapshot & Backup
snapshot-backup.summary: Before upgrading any component, snapshot and back it up. If a phase fails, restore the component to its previous version from the snapshot/backup. This is your rollback safety net for every later phase.
checklist: VMware Cloud Director: snapshot the appliance and back up the embedded database.
vSphere Replication: snapshot the appliance VM.
Live Site Recovery: snapshot the appliance and back up the embedded vPostgres database.
vCenter: VM snapshot + file-based backup from the vCenter interface.
NSX Manager: back up the NSX Manager configuration.
ESXi and vSAN: back up the ESXi host configuration (KB 2042141).
Aria Operations for Networks: snapshot (KB 55829) and back up the deployment.
Aria Automation Orchestrator: VM snapshot — deactivate 'Snapshot the virtual machine's memory' (8.x does not support memory snapshots).
Aria Operations for Logs / Aria Operations: snapshot the cluster nodes.
Telco Cloud Automation: snapshot the TCA appliance.
Avi Load Balancer: snapshot the Avi controllers.

vcd.title: Upgrade VMware Cloud Director to {target}
vcd.summary: First phase for VNF (Cloud Director Edition) deployments. Upgrade the Cloud Director cells and embedded database to {target}. The detailed click-by-click procedure lives in the official documentation — use the link below as the source of truth.
prerequisites: Superuser credentials for all Cloud Director cells in the server group.
All ESXi hosts enabled (deactivated ESXi hosts are unsupported since Cloud Director 10.3.2a).
Embedded database backed up and a snapshot of the primary appliance taken.
vcd.impact: The tenant portal and API are briefly unavailable while the cells are upgraded.
vcd.rollback: Restore the primary appliance snapshot and the embedded database backup.
considerations: If vCenter disconnects after the upgrade, reconnect via Resources > Infrastructure Resources > vCenter Server Instances > select instance > Reconnect (KB78885).
If Avi Load Balancer was deployed as part of Cloud Director, upgrade Avi Load Balancer as well.

vsphere-replication.title: Upgrade vSphere Replication to {target}
vsphere-replication.summary: Upgrade the vSphere Replication environment to {target} before Live Site Recovery. Follow the official procedure for the exact steps.
prerequisites: Snapshot of the vSphere Replication appliance VM taken.
vsphere-replication.impact: Replication is paused during the upgrade.
considerations: Upgrade ALL components on the protected site before the recovery site.
If the protected site becomes inaccessible during the upgrade, use the recovery site to restore it to its original state.

lsr.title: Upgrade Live Site Recovery to {target}
lsr.summary: Upgrade Live Site Recovery (formerly Site Recovery Manager) to {target}. Complete all tasks on the protected site first, then the recovery site. See the official procedure for the steps.
prerequisites: Snapshot of the LSR virtual appliance taken.
Reviewed the Prerequisites and Best Practices for Live Site Recovery Upgrade.
vSphere Replication already upgraded to {target}.
lsr.impact: Live Site Recovery is unavailable until BOTH vSphere Replication and LSR are upgraded to {target} on the protected and recovery sites.
lsr.rollback: Restore the appliance snapshot and the embedded vPostgres database backup.
considerations: Complete the protected site fully before starting the recovery site.

tca.title: Upgrade Telco Cloud Automation to {target}
tca.summary: Upgrade TCA Manager and TCA Control Plane to {target}. The supported source TCA releases vary by target TCP version — see the official guide's 'supports the following Telco Cloud Automation upgrade paths' list for the exact set. The points below are the planning context and order.
prerequisites: Components of the current TCA release are activated.
TCA Manager and TCA Control Plane appliances are backed up.
The TCA {target} upgrade bundle is downloaded from the TCP Advanced downloads page.
tca.impact: The TCA management plane is unavailable during the upgrade; running CNFs are unaffected.
tca.rollback: Restore the TCA Manager and TCA Control Plane appliance snapshots.
considerations: If an Airgap server exists, upgrade it to {target} first.
After upgrade, verify the dashboard shows {target} and all services are up.
Update the existing Harbor with ca.crt under TCA Manager > Infrastructure > Partner Systems (private CA-signed/self-signed certificates only).
Calico add-on update may appear on workload clusters after 3.2 → 3.3.0.1 — recommended, but may cause a minor service fluctuation.

Your TCA source version is implied by the TCP source release you picked in this planner (see the Components matrix' TCA row) — there's no separate TCA-version selector. If your live environment's TCA patch differs from that mapping, treat the TCP source closest to your actual TCA release as authoritative and cross-check the official TCA upgrade paths list.

snippets.label: Verify services are up (browser)
snippets.note: Confirm both URLs load after the upgrade.

airgap.title: Upgrade Airgap Server to {target}
airgap.summary: The Airgap Server serves images and Helm charts to disconnected environments. Upgrade it to TCA {target} before any Tanzu Kubernetes cluster upgrade. Follow the 'Upgrade Airgap Appliance' procedure in the docs.
considerations: Ensure required Helm charts and images are published in the local repository before upgrading dependent components.

harbor.title: Upgrade Harbor for CNFs to {target}
harbor.summary: Harbor is the registry for CNF Helm charts and container images, deployed and upgraded as a CNF through TCA. Follow the Harbor for CNFs Deployment and Configuration Guide for the upgrade procedure.
considerations: Upgrading Harbor through TCA is only supported if Harbor was originally deployed as a CNF using TCA (starting with TCP 4.0.1) — not for Harbor instances deployed outside TCA or on TCP releases earlier than 4.0.1.
Download Harbor for CNFs from Drivers & Tools on the TCP Advanced downloads page.

snippets.label: Airgap only — push Harbor CNF charts/images to the Airgap Harbor registry
snippets.note: Reference snippet from the Harbor for CNFs guide — verify against the linked doc. The harborcnf project must be pre-created; pass.txt holds the airgap Harbor password, ca.crt its certificate.

avi.title: Upgrade Avi Load Balancer to {target}
avi.summary: Upgrade Avi Load Balancer (formerly NSX Advanced Load Balancer) to {target}. The detailed steps are in the Avi Load Balancer Upgrade Guide.
prerequisites: Snapshot and backup of the Avi controllers taken.
Avi Load Balancer {target} is compatible with your AKO version (see the AKO Compatibility Matrix).
avi.impact: Briefly affects load-balanced application traffic during controller upgrade.
considerations: Download Avi Load Balancer from the VMware Avi Load Balancer downloads page.
After upgrade, confirm applications remain accessible.

ako.title: Upgrade Avi Kubernetes Operator (AKO) to {target}
ako.summary: AKO integrates Avi Load Balancer with Kubernetes clusters. Upgrade it to {target} after Avi Load Balancer. If AKO was installed with Helm, upgrade it with Helm (see the Upgrade AKO section in Install AKO).
prerequisites: Avi Load Balancer already upgraded.
AKO {target} is compatible with your Kubernetes versions (AKO Compatibility Matrix).
Airgap + Helm: charts published in the local repository.
considerations: Download AKO from the VMware Avi Load Balancer downloads page.

tkg-mgmt.title: Upgrade Tanzu Kubernetes Management Cluster to {target}
tkg-mgmt.summary: TCA is integrated with Tanzu Kubernetes Grid. Upgrade the management clusters first (before AKO and the workload clusters) to {target}. Follow 'Upgrade the Kubernetes Version for a Cluster Instance' for the exact steps.
prerequisites: If airgapped, the Airgap server is already upgraded.
Kubernetes upgrade compatibility verified for each management cluster.
Latest Kubernetes Cluster templates downloaded; Cluster OVA imported into vSphere and converted to a template.
tkg-mgmt.impact: Rolling control-plane/node replacement on the management cluster.
tkg-mgmt.rollback: Follow TKG/TCA cluster recovery guidance; clusters are upgraded in place.
considerations: Upgrade each Tanzu Kubernetes management cluster sequentially before proceeding.

Large Kubernetes version jumps (e.g. from an older TCP release) may not be a single hop — TCA's 'Upgrade Management Cluster Kubernetes Version' compatibility table lists mandatory intermediate versions. Check it against your current cluster version before starting.
A management cluster crossing Kubernetes 1.27 moves its node OS from Photon OS 3 to Photon OS 5 — see the Tanzu Kubernetes Workload Cluster phase for the CNF/CSAR impact.
Certificate renewal is automatically enabled (90-day default) whenever a management cluster is upgraded.

Before starting: customizations from previous CNF instantiate/upgrade operations on nodepools are preserved, but manual changes made directly on nodes are not. Applications may see downtime and clusters may take time to become available during the upgrade. Control-plane and worker node IP addresses change after upgrade. If an upgrade fails, correct the configuration and retry.

tkg-workload.title: Upgrade Tanzu Kubernetes Workload Cluster to {target}
tkg-workload.summary: After the management clusters (and AKO, if installed) are upgraded, upgrade each Tanzu Kubernetes workload cluster to {target}. Follow 'Upgrade Kubernetes Version for Workload Cluster Instance' for the exact steps.
prerequisites: Management clusters already upgraded.
AKO upgraded first if it is installed in the workload cluster.
Kubernetes upgrade compatibility verified for each workload cluster.
tkg-workload.impact: Rolling node replacement; workloads should stay available if correctly configured.
tkg-workload.rollback: Follow TKG/TCA cluster recovery guidance; clusters are upgraded in place.
considerations: Upgrade each workload cluster, then confirm all pods are up and running.
After upgrade, update the Kubernetes version in the cluster templates: TCA UI > Infrastructure > CaaS Infrastructure > Cluster templates (edit templates showing compatibility errors).

Large Kubernetes version jumps may not be a single hop — TCA's 'Upgrade Workload Cluster Kubernetes Version' compatibility table lists mandatory intermediate versions, staged alongside the management cluster's progress.

A workload cluster crossing Kubernetes 1.27 moves its node OS from Photon OS 3 to Photon OS 5 (TCA 'Migrating from Photon OS 3 to Photon OS 5'). CNFs with custom kernel packages/drivers (DPDK, SR-IOV) need CSAR updates: vfio_pci.disable_resets is renamed vfio_pci_core.disable_resets on Photon 5, driver package naming changes, and CSARs should carry conditional infra_requirements per OS during the transition.
Certificate renewal is automatically enabled (90-day default) whenever a workload cluster is upgraded.

Check and upgrade the required node pools in the workload cluster. Control-plane and worker node IP addresses change after upgrade, and applications may see downtime while the cluster is unavailable. If an upgrade fails, correct the configuration and retry.

nsx.title: Upgrade NSX to {target}
nsx.summary: Upgrade NSX to {target} using the Upgrade Coordinator in NSX Manager. It orchestrates the upgrade in order: NSX Edges → ESXi hosts → NSX management plane. The official NSX upgrade procedure has the detailed steps.
prerequisites: NSX pre-upgrade tasks performed and the NSX Upgrade Checklist reviewed.
Infrastructure prepared for the NSX upgrade.
Existing NSX Manager backed up.
All alerts on the NSX Alerts page fixed.
NSX downloaded from the TCP Advanced downloads page.
nsx.impact: Edge and host upgrades are rolling; brief data-path impact possible per host.
nsx.rollback: Restore the NSX Manager backup; follow NSX Troubleshooting Upgrade Failures.
considerations: SR-IOV / non-migratable worker nodes: drain pods and power off the worker VMs before the ESXi host step (see the ESXi phase).
vSphere Lifecycle Manager-enabled clusters: follow 'Upgrade a vSphere Lifecycle Manager-enabled Cluster'.
After upgrade: verify clusters are in Success state (NSX Manager VIP > System > Fabric > Nodes > Host Transport Nodes); reconfigure with CONFIGURE NSX if a host errors.
Update the NSX certificate in TCA Appliance Manager (TCA-CP): Configuration > NSX > Edit > re-enter password > accept certificate > Save.

vcenter.title: Upgrade vCenter Server to {target}
vcenter.summary: Upgrade vCenter for the management domain first, then each resource/workload domain, to {target} (any patch on top of 8.0 U3). See 'Patching and Updating vCenter Server 8.0 Deployments' for the steps.
prerequisites: VAMI shows all vCenter services healthy and started.
VM snapshot of the vCenter appliance taken.
vcenter.impact: vCenter management is briefly unavailable; running workloads are unaffected.
vcenter.rollback: Restore the vCenter VM snapshot / file-based backup.
considerations: You can also update vCenter through Telco Cloud Automation (Infrastructure Lifecycle Management Workflows).

esxi.title: Upgrade ESXi to {target}
esxi.summary: Upgrade ESXi after vCenter to {target} (any patch on top of 8.0 U3). vSAN is upgraded automatically with the host. The procedure differs for hosts running Tanzu Kubernetes worker nodes that use SR-IOV vs hosts without SR-IOV — see the official ESXi Upgrade Guide for full detail.
prerequisites: vCenter already upgraded.
ESXi downloaded from the TCP Advanced downloads page.
esxi.impact: All ESXi hosts in the cluster are rebooted during the upgrade.
esxi.rollback: Restore the ESXi host configuration backup (KB 2042141).
considerations: Hosts with SR-IOV (or non-migratable) worker nodes: drain pods and power off those worker VMs, remediate the host in Lifecycle Manager, then power on, uncordon, and resume health checks.
Hosts without SR-IOV: upgrade via GUI installer, Scripts, ESXCLI, vSphere Auto Deploy, or vSphere Lifecycle Manager.
vSphere LCM-enabled clusters: follow 'Upgrade a vSphere Lifecycle Manager-enabled Cluster'.

snippets.label: SR-IOV hosts — drain & pause a worker node (ccli on TCA-CP)
snippets.note: Reference snippet from the guide — verify against the linked doc. Run after SSHing into the TCA-CP VM for the relevant Management Cluster.
commands: ccliPodName=`kubectl get pods -A | grep ccli | awk '{ print $2 }'`

snippets.label: SR-IOV hosts — after remediation: uncordon & resume health check
snippets.note: Reference snippet from the guide — verify against the linked doc.

vsan.title: Upgrade vSAN Cluster to {target}
vsan.summary: vSAN is upgraded automatically when you upgrade ESXi hosts. After all hosts are upgraded, optionally upgrade the vSAN cluster objects (on-disk format and HCL DB). See the official vSAN documentation.
vsan.impact: No additional reboot beyond the ESXi upgrade.
vsan.rollback: Disk format upgrade is one-way; a previous disk format keeps running, so defer it if unsure.
considerations: The disk format upgrade is optional — a previous on-disk format keeps running, but the latest format provides the complete vSAN feature set.
Update the vSAN HCL database and verify the cluster is healthy after the upgrade.

aria-orchestrator.title: Upgrade Aria Automation Orchestrator to {target}
aria-orchestrator.summary: Final CNF phase. Upgrade Aria Automation Orchestrator (formerly vRealize Orchestrator) to {target}. See the Aria Automation Orchestrator Upgrade Guide for the steps.
prerequisites: VM snapshot taken with memory snapshot deactivated (8.x does not support memory snapshots).

aria-products.title: Upgrade Aria Products
aria-products.summary: Upgrade the VMware Aria products in order: Aria Operations for Logs, Aria Operations, Aria Automation Orchestrator, then Aria Operations for Networks. Check compatible versions in the VMware Interoperability Matrix and follow each product's upgrade guide.
prerequisites: Snapshots taken of all Aria deployments (Orchestrator with memory snapshot deactivated; Aria Operations for Networks per KB 55829).
considerations: Aria Operations for Logs — also upgrade the agents in the required VMs to capture data.
Aria Operations — the NTP service does not start automatically after upgrade and can fail to bring the cluster online; apply KB93793 workaround.
Aria Operations for Networks — continue using 6.13.

Sequencing: the guide's 'Upgrade Sequence for VNF Workloads' table places Aria Operations for Networks earlier than the other three products here — concurrently with the NSX phase (its own numbered step), while Aria Operations, Aria Operations for Logs, and Aria Automation Orchestrator are sequenced after vSAN. This card follows the guide's shared 'Upgrade Aria Products' procedure and bundles all four together; if you're following the table's exact per-component order, do the Aria Operations for Networks portion alongside your NSX phase instead.

post-upgrade.title: Post-Upgrade Verification
post-upgrade.summary: After all phases, verify the platform is healthy across infrastructure, compute, storage, and the VIM/NFVI components.
checklist: Network: VLANs inter-routable; ESXi uplinks functioning; MTU correct on physical switches; NTP/DHCP/DNS reachable; external connectivity and external Harbor (if used) reachable.
Compute: log in to each host client; no warnings or alerts on the Host Client UI.
Storage: vSAN accessible and healthy.

NFVI & VIM — vCenter: management and resource vCenter Servers accessible; cluster properties correct; all vSphere Distributed Switches working with matching MTU; root password expiry per policy; NTP configured on all TCP components.
NFVI & VIM — VMware Cloud Director: accessible via Avi Load Balancer and individual cell FQDN; registered with vCenter and configured with NSX.

NFVI & VIM — NSX: Manager and component connection status green; no critical alerts or warnings; all edge nodes and ESXi hosts connected and operational; ESXi/edge node password expiry and account policies (admin, root, audit) meet company/security policy; TEP connectivity validated; sufficient TEP IP pools (edge and host) available for future deployments.
Airgap Server: accessible, and its Harbor server is ready with all repositories and Helm charts.
Aria Automation Orchestrator: accessible, and you can run workflows.

Tanzu Kubernetes Cluster: all pods in the management and workload clusters are up and running; users can instantiate new CNFs and deploy new Tanzu Kubernetes clusters; run diagnostics on clusters through the TCA UI; verify and fix alerts on the TCA dashboard.
Operations Management — Aria Operations for Logs: accessible; syslog data is actively collected and stored; configured content pack components are accessible.
Operations Management — Aria Operations: accessible; all configured management components are accessible.
Operations Management — Aria Operations for Networks: accessible; network data is actively received and stored.

Business Continuity & DR — Live Site Recovery: successfully upgraded on the protected (management cluster) and recovery sites and the sites are paired; the pair appears on the LSR home page; LSR is accessible from both sites.

Business Continuity & DR — vSphere Replication: successfully upgraded on the protected (management pod) and recovery sites and the sites are paired; vSphere Replication servers are registered with their respective vCenter Servers on both sites.
Delete Snapshots: once every component above is verified working as expected, delete the snapshots taken before the upgrade.
